bins.name: State Authority Must Be Preserved
bins.desc: OMS opposes FERC asserting jurisdiction over large-load interconnection as an intrusion on state retail service authority. The ANOPR offers no evidence that existing state processes are deficient, and the proposed reclassification risks severing utilities' obligation to serve, creating regulatory gaps, and triggering legal challenges. OMS prefers voluntary cooperative federalism over mandatory federal rules.
quotes: load interconnection study processes have been working well within the MISO footprint and there are no bottlenecks for interconnecting and energizing large loads that need attention in MISO.

it is essential that entities causing transmission impacts – directly or indirectly – bear their share of costs, and that states retain full visibility into how such determinations are made and costs are allocated.

If outcomes of this proceeding cause regulatory gaps, unintended consequences, or are or not in alignment with the FPA (and other long-standing systems of cooperative federalism established in settled case law that have governed the electric grid for decades) the desired speed of interconnecting large loads could come to a standstill through unintended system inefficiencies, new bottlenecks, and most likely – legal challenges.

The ANOPR's proposed framework would effectively reclassify new large-load interconnections as FERC-jurisdictional transmission. This shift could have far-reaching implications for the division of regulatory authority between states and the federal government.
OMS is concerned that, as proposed, the ANOPR could sever the existing link between load interconnection and the utility's obligation to serve.
the ANOPR provides no evidence or examples demonstrating that current state- or utility-administered interconnection processes are discriminatory or inadequate.

Rather than imposing federal jurisdiction, the Commission should pursue a cooperative federalism approach by developing voluntary best practices with states to improve interconnection efficiency, requiring load interconnection processes that enhance transparency, and other novel approaches to meet the moment.
the Commission should limit its authority over large-load interconnection requests to the study process itself, with clear guardrails ensuring no effect on retail service obligations.

FERC should require large loads seeking to interconnect to demonstrate: site control, proof of transmission service, state notification and consultation, and disconnection authority if service obligations are not met.
guardrails are needed to prevent inadvertent reliance on other authorities, such as FPA Section 202(c) emergency powers.

bins.name: Raise the 20 MW Threshold
bins.desc: OMS argues the proposed 20 MW minimum is too low and would swamp the queue with smaller customers, defeating the efficiency goals of the ANOPR. Existing state large-load tariffs use thresholds of 100 to 150 MW, which OMS views as a more appropriate benchmark.
quotes: Defining large loads as any interconnecting customer demanding a minimum of 20 MW would likely limit any efficiencies or benefits intended by the ANOPR due to the sheer number of prospective large loads.
many other states have approved or are considering large load tariffs that all contemplate a range of 100-150 MW.

bins.name: No Automatic Wholesale Self-Supply Right
bins.desc: OMS urges FERC to confirm that interconnection approval does not automatically grant large loads the right to self-procure from wholesale markets, limiting any such access to approved co-location or behind-the-meter arrangements.
quotes: FERC should confirm that interconnection under this framework does not confer a right to self-procure from wholesale markets, except through approved arrangements such as co-location or behind-the-meter generation used for flexibility or demand-response purposes.

bins.name: Thorough Study Before Connection
bins.desc: OMS supports thorough transmission-level study requirements and argues that connecting large loads without adequate analysis of resource adequacy and cost allocation creates reliability risks. Any FERC role should be confined to the study process itself.
quotes: connecting large loads to the bulk power system without thorough study, without sufficient resource support, and without appropriate cost alignment could introduce new reliability risks and inequities across customer classes.
the Commission should limit its authority over large-load interconnection requests to the study process itself, with clear guardrails ensuring no effect on retail service obligations.

FERC should require large loads seeking to interconnect to demonstrate: site control, proof of transmission service, state notification and consultation, and disconnection authority if service obligations are not met.

bins.name: Cost Causation for Large Loads
bins.desc: OMS insists that entities causing transmission impacts must bear their share of costs and that states retain visibility into cost allocation decisions. Co-location and flexibility rules must also ensure large loads pay for the full range of grid services they use.
quotes: it is essential that entities causing transmission impacts – directly or indirectly – bear their share of costs, and that states retain full visibility into how such determinations are made and costs are allocated.

connecting large loads to the bulk power system without thorough study, without sufficient resource support, and without appropriate cost alignment could introduce new reliability risks and inequities across customer classes.

as the Commission develops proposed requirements for flexible or co-located loads, it should ensure potential changes to existing regulatory constructs, public engagement processes, cost allocation frameworks, wholesale markets, and transmission planning are sufficiently stringent to shield existing customers from negative impacts and to determine whether the large loads are paying for the grid services they use (transmission, ancillary services, reliability backstop, etc.).

bins.name: Co-location With Customer Protection
bins.desc: OMS appreciates incentives for co-located generation as a way to reduce upgrade costs and protect existing customers, but calls for stringent rules ensuring co-located and flexible loads pay for all grid services and do not shift costs onto ratepayers.
quotes: FERC should confirm that interconnection under this framework does not confer a right to self-procure from wholesale markets, except through approved arrangements such as co-location or behind-the-meter generation used for flexibility or demand-response purposes.
enabling load flexibility is critical to accelerating the interconnection of large loads while maintaining a reliable and affordable grid for existing customers.

as the Commission develops proposed requirements for flexible or co-located loads, it should ensure potential changes to existing regulatory constructs, public engagement processes, cost allocation frameworks, wholesale markets, and transmission planning are sufficiently stringent to shield existing customers from negative impacts and to determine whether the large loads are paying for the grid services they use (transmission, ancillary services, reliability backstop, etc.).

bins.name: Flexibility to Speed Interconnection
bins.desc: OMS views load flexibility as critical to accelerating interconnection while keeping the grid affordable and reliable for existing customers. It conditions support on adequate cost allocation safeguards.
quotes: FERC should confirm that interconnection under this framework does not confer a right to self-procure from wholesale markets, except through approved arrangements such as co-location or behind-the-meter generation used for flexibility or demand-response purposes.
enabling load flexibility is critical to accelerating the interconnection of large loads while maintaining a reliable and affordable grid for existing customers.

as the Commission develops proposed requirements for flexible or co-located loads, it should ensure potential changes to existing regulatory constructs, public engagement processes, cost allocation frameworks, wholesale markets, and transmission planning are sufficiently stringent to shield existing customers from negative impacts and to determine whether the large loads are paying for the grid services they use (transmission, ancillary services, reliability backstop, etc.).

bins.name: MISO Processes Already Work
bins.desc: OMS notes that MISO has no interconnection backlog for large loads and that existing processes, including the Expedited Project Review, already coordinate transmission, resource adequacy, and state oversight effectively. Any federal rule should account for this regional context.
quotes: load interconnection study processes have been working well within the MISO footprint and there are no bottlenecks for interconnecting and energizing large loads that need attention in MISO.
many other states have approved or are considering large load tariffs that all contemplate a range of 100-150 MW.

bins.name: Cooperative Federalism Over Mandates
bins.desc: OMS argues the most durable outcome would result from voluntary best practices developed with states rather than federal mandates. The ANOPR's premise that asserting federal jurisdiction leaves state authority intact is likely incorrect, and the FPA's historical federal-state balance should guide any final rule.
quotes: If outcomes of this proceeding cause regulatory gaps, unintended consequences, or are or not in alignment with the FPA (and other long-standing systems of cooperative federalism established in settled case law that have governed the electric grid for decades) the desired speed of interconnecting large loads could come to a standstill through unintended system inefficiencies, new bottlenecks, and most likely – legal challenges.

The ANOPR's proposed framework would effectively reclassify new large-load interconnections as FERC-jurisdictional transmission. This shift could have far-reaching implications for the division of regulatory authority between states and the federal government.
the ANOPR provides no evidence or examples demonstrating that current state- or utility-administered interconnection processes are discriminatory or inadequate.

Rather than imposing federal jurisdiction, the Commission should pursue a cooperative federalism approach by developing voluntary best practices with states to improve interconnection efficiency, requiring load interconnection processes that enhance transparency, and other novel approaches to meet the moment.

bins.name: Utility Obligation to Serve at Risk
bins.desc: OMS warns that shifting interconnection approval to FERC would sever the link between interconnection and the incumbent utility's obligation to serve, leaving unclear which entity is responsible for reliability and capacity adequacy over time.
quotes: OMS is concerned that, as proposed, the ANOPR could sever the existing link between load interconnection and the utility's obligation to serve.

bins.name: Reliability Risk From Rushed Interconnection
bins.desc: OMS cautions that connecting large loads without adequate study, resource support, or cost alignment could introduce new reliability risks and inequities across customer classes.
quotes: connecting large loads to the bulk power system without thorough study, without sufficient resource support, and without appropriate cost alignment could introduce new reliability risks and inequities across customer classes.

bins.name: Explicit Guardrails Required
bins.desc: OMS calls for specific procedural guardrails including mandatory site control, state notification, designated load-serving entities for resource adequacy, and protections against invoking emergency powers to fill gaps created by an insufficiently studied interconnection process.
quotes: FERC should require large loads seeking to interconnect to demonstrate: site control, proof of transmission service, state notification and consultation, and disconnection authority if service obligations are not met.
guardrails are needed to prevent inadvertent reliance on other authorities, such as FPA Section 202(c) emergency powers.
